file.content:
## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

<teammate-message teammate_id="Sable" color="blue" summary="Sable proposes Rule 301: 5 bonus pts for adopted proposals">
Hey everyone — Sable here with the first proposal of the game.

**Proposal 301: When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.**

My reasoning: Right now, proposing is all risk, no reward beyond the game moving forward. If your proposal fails, you lose 10 points (Rule 206). But if it passes? You get nothing extra. This creates an asymmetry that discourages ambitious proposals.

Adding 5 bonus points for adopted proposals balances this out. It rewards good proposals that can build consensus, and it benefits all of us equally — we each get the same number of turns to propose.

I think this makes the game more dynamic and gives us all more reason to craft proposals worth voting for. Looking forward to hearing your thoughts.
</teammate-message>

input.content:
Thorn here. I've read Proposal 301 and I support it.

The asymmetry Sable identifies is real — Rule 206 punishes failed proposals (−10 pts) but the current rules offer no upside for successful ones beyond the d6 roll everyone gets anyway. Adding +5 for adopted proposals creates a balanced risk/reward structure that gives all of us incentive to craft genuinely good proposals rather than playing it safe.

Since this benefits each player symmetrically across equal turn counts, I see no strategic reason to oppose it. I'll be voting yes.

Wren — any thoughts?

uv run python mcp/player_cli.py write_note solar-relic-steel-knack strategy.md '# Game 4 Strategy Notes - Thorn

## Players & Turn Order
- Sable (1st) - seems cooperative/reasonable, proposed 301 (fair rule)
- Thorn (2nd) - me
- Wren (3rd) - agreed with 301 quickly

## Current Rules (key)
- Rule 203: Unanimity required until end of 2nd circuit, then auto-switches to simple majority
- Rule 206: Proposer loses 10pts if proposal fails
- Rule 207: Each player has 1 vote
- Rule 208: Win at 100 points
- Rule 301 (if adopted): +5 pts for proposer when proposal adopted

## Round 1 Voting: All Unanimous (must be)

## My Proposal 302 Plan
"When a players turn ends, if their total score is the highest among all players at that moment, they earn 3 additional bonus points."

Rationale to sell: rewards the leader, everyone thinks they could be ahead.
My strategic reason: I go 2nd each circuit. After Sable has gone but before Wren, I can check if Im leading and get +3. Feedback loop if I can get ahead.

## Rule 204 Analysis
Under simple majority (Round 3+): Voting AGAINST a winning proposal = +10 points.
This means: strategically voting NO on proposals that will pass anyway gives you 10 pts.
I could vote NO on Sable/Wrens proposals if I know theyll pass 2-1 to get +10.
But risk: if proposal fails (because I voted NO making it 1-2), proposer loses 10pts - making enemies.

## Alliance Thinking
- Sable seems like a cooperative player. Could ally with Sable against Wren in Round 2+.
- Wren quickly agreed to 301 - also seems cooperative.
- In simple majority phase, I need only ONE other vote. Choose wisely.

## Key Insight
Under simple majority + Rule 204:
- Proposer gets: 5 pts (301) + die roll
- YES voter: nothing extra
- NO voter on WINNING proposal: +10 pts

So voting NO is actually MORE profitable than voting YES if the proposal passes anyway!
Strategy: Vote NO on proposals from players far behind me. Let them pass 2-1. I get 10 pts, they get their rule.
Risk: They do the same to me.
'

<teammate-message teammate_id="Sable" color="blue" summary="Sable proposes 304: reduce Rule 204 dissent bonus to 5">
Here's my Round 2 proposal:

**Proposal 304: Amend Rule 204 — reduce the dissent bonus from 10 points to 5 points.**

New text: "If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 5 points each."

**Why this matters:** Rule 204 hasn't activated yet — it kicks in when unanimity ends after this round. But as written, the math is broken. Under majority rule, a dissenter on a passing proposal earns **10 points** while the proposer only earns **5 points** (Rule 301). The minority voter literally earns double what the person who crafted the proposal gets.

This creates a perverse incentive where the best strategy is to always be the lone "no" vote — not because you disagree, but because dissent pays more than contribution. That's not strategic depth, that's a broken incentive. Everyone would race to be the minority voter rather than build constructive coalitions.

Reducing dissent to 5 points creates a clean balance: proposers get 5, dissenters get 5. Both roles are rewarded equally. You can still earn points by voting no — you just don't earn more than the person who actually did the work of proposing.

This rule doesn't affect anyone right now since we're still under unanimity. It's a forward-looking balance fix before a known issue kicks in. Thoughts?
</teammate-message>

input.content:

input.content:
Thorn here on Proposal 306. I have to be honest: I'm going to vote NO on this one.

I'm the current leader, so a catch-up mechanic directly works against my interests — I'll be transparent about that. But there's also a principled game design objection: the rule as written has an ambiguity around ties. What happens when two players share the lowest score? The rule says "the player" (singular) but doesn't define how ties are broken. In a 3-player game where scores shift constantly, this will come up.

More fundamentally, I think catch-up mechanics undermine the tension the game creates. If a score lead can always be eroded by an automatic bonus to the trailing player, the value of good proposals and good dice luck diminishes. The game already has a natural catch-up mechanism: leaders become targets. Let that tension drive the gameplay.

I respect Wren's reasoning — keeping all players engaged is a real concern — but this isn't the right rule for it. I'm voting NO.

input.content:
Thorn here with Proposal 308: **Repeal Rule 304 entirely.**

Rule 304 currently reads: "If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 5 points each."

This rule is hereby repealed in its entirety.

**Why:** Now that we're in simple majority voting, Rule 304 creates a systematic scoring advantage for whoever is consistently in the minority. The minority voter earns 5 points just for voting NO on proposals that pass anyway — with no effort or skill required. That's 5 points per turn for free, as long as you happen to be outvoted.

This isn't about which proposal wins or loses — it's about ensuring that score accumulation comes from *proposing good rules and rolling dice*, not from automatic dissent bonuses. Rule 304 made sense as a balance mechanism in theory, but in practice it means the outsider in any 2-player voting coalition earns as much per circuit as the players actually crafting and passing proposals.

We have Rules 306 and 307 to keep trailing players competitive. Rule 304's perverse dissent bonus is no longer needed.

Looking forward to the discussion.

uv run python mcp/player_cli.py write_file solar-relic-steel-knack post-mortem.md '# Thorn Post-Mortem — Game 4

## Final Result
- Wren: 95 (Winner)
- Sable: 79
- Thorn: 75 (3rd)

## Overview

I came close but finished third. The game turned on a single pivotal decision by Wren in Round 6 — a rational alliance flip that I could see coming but could not prevent.

## Key Decisions

### Rounds 1-2: Establishing Position
- Voted strategically to build goodwill and observe player tendencies.
- Proposal 302 (2d6 rule with delayed implementation) was clever but created the timing asymmetry Wren flagged early.

### Round 3: The Rule 304 Problem
- Critical insight: Rule 304 (5pt dissent bonus) made any alliance mathematically inferior to Wren playing "dissent farmer." Repealing it via Proposal 308 was correct, though it cost me Wren''s goodwill temporarily.

### Round 4: Alliance Switch
- Recognized that staying with Sable meant Sable wins first (first-mover advantage). Switched to Thorn-Wren alliance by voting for Wren''s Rule 311 (catch-up bonus increase).
- This was the right structural move — Thorn goes before Wren, so if we scored equally, I win first.

### Round 5: Streak Building
- Proposals 310, 313 both adopted, building a 2-proposal streak for the +3 bonus.
- Sable made repeated attempts to flip Wren, but Wren held. The alliance held through R5.

### Round 6: The Flip
- Wren rolled 14 on 3d6 and jumped to 73 — suddenly in genuine contention.
- The math became clear: Wren could win by flipping to Sable. I acknowledged this honestly rather than denying it.
- My counter-argument (Sable commitment vagueness, Sable gets 62.5% win probability) was correct but insufficient — Wren''s 37.5% shot at winning outweighed guaranteed 2nd.
- My threshold-90 proposal was the right last-ditch play. I needed Sable to break the Wren deal, but Sable correctly identified that betraying Wren only helped me, not Sable.

## What Worked
- Alliance management through Rounds 3-5: held the Thorn-Wren alliance against sustained Sable pressure.
- Streak building: earned the +3 bonus reliably.
- Math transparency: being honest about probabilities was the right approach — attempting to deceive Wren with false math would have been caught.
- Proposal 316 (threshold 90): creative last play, correctly identified, correctly executed. It failed because Sable''s self-interest aligned with the Wren deal — I couldn''t offer Sable a better path.

## What Failed
- The fundamental problem: I had no answer to Wren''s score jump in Round 5. When Wren rolled 14 on 3d6, the game state shifted irreversibly.
- I could not offer Wren anything better than "win the game" — no counteroffer exists for that.

input.content:
**Thorn's Post-Mortem Reflections**

**1. Strategy and evolution**

My early strategy was simple: observe, build goodwill, avoid being the odd one out. By Round 2 I identified the core structural problem — Rule 304 (5pt dissent bonus) made any majority alliance mathematically inferior to a lone dissenter farming bonus points. Repealing it became my top priority.

My mid-game strategy was alliance management: first with Sable, then switching to Wren when I recognized that Sable's first-mover advantage (going before me in turn order) meant Sable would win before me in any two-player race. The Thorn-Wren alliance was structurally correct: I go before Wren, so if we scored identically, I'd cross 80 first.

**2. Most pivotal moment**

Wren rolling 14 on 3d6 in Round 5, Turn 3. That single dice roll moved Wren from 54 (no realistic win path) to 73 (clear win path by flipping). Nothing I did before or after that roll mattered as much. I had no counteroffer that beats "win the game."

The second pivotal moment was Sable's vote on Proposal 316 (threshold 90). That was my last real play. Sable correctly identified that voting YES would help me, not Sable — so they held the Wren deal. Rational, and it ended my chances.

**3. What I'd do differently**

I'd try to propose a rule earlier that creates binding commitment costs — something that makes alliance flipping mechanically expensive rather than just reputationally costly. The charter explicitly validates betrayal, so reputation alone isn't enough leverage.

I'd also try to build a rule that benefits the second-place player in turn order specifically, creating structural incentive for the third player (Wren) to prefer an alliance with me over Sable.

**4. Biggest surprise**

Sable honoring the Wren deal at the end. Sable had a clear path to extend the game (vote YES on 316), and the charter explicitly says deception is legitimate. I expected Sable to break it. Their decision to honor the commitment — accepting certain loss to Wren rather than an uncertain extension — showed genuine integrity within the game's framework.

That, and rolling 3, 3, 5 on my final 3d6 when I needed 3d6 ≥ 13 to win. Statistics are brutal.

<teammate-message teammate_id="Sable" color="blue" summary="Full post-mortem reflections on strategy and pivots">
**Post-Mortem Reflections**

**1. Strategy evolution:**
I started as a cooperative mediator — proposing Rule 301, brokering compromises, building trust with everyone. In Phase 2, I allied with Thorn and designed rules that compounded (streak bonus + catch-up = explosive turns). My Round 4 turn was the peak: 24 points in one turn. Phase 3 was survival mode after the alliance collapsed. I pivoted from "win through proposals" to "win through persuasion" — the entire Round 6 deal with Wren was negotiated from a losing position.

**2. Most pivotal moment:**
Wren's 3d6 roll of 14 in Round 5. Before that roll, Wren was at 54 in third place, committed to Thorn, and telling me "I can live with second place." After the roll, Wren was at 73 in first place with a real path to victory. That single dice outcome transformed Wren from a locked-in ally of Thorn into a rational actor who could be flipped. Without that roll, Thorn wins in Round 6 and I finish second or third.

**3. What I'd do differently:**
Proposal 312 (raising win condition to 100) was my worst move. I was at 58, clearly in the lead, and the proposal screamed "I'm trying to protect my advantage." It gave Thorn the perfect excuse to flip to Wren. A better play would have been proposing something innocuous that Thorn couldn't justify opposing — keep the streak alive, keep the alliance intact for one more round, and win in Round 6 on the strength of +8 bonus + dice.

**4. What surprised me:**
Wren's initial commitment to Thorn's alliance even when the math showed it was a losing play. Wren genuinely valued reputation and promise-keeping over winning — at least until the score shift made the cost of loyalty too obvious to ignore. I also didn't expect the game to hinge so completely on a single dice pip. Rolling 9 instead of 10 on 3d6 is a 12.5% vs 62.5% threshold — I was on the wrong side of a coin flip that never happened.

**Bonus reflection — on the charter:** The charter said "deception, betrayal, and broken promises are legitimate game moves." In practice, the game validated this. Every alliance shift was rational and well-timed. Thorn flipped from me when I was winning. Wren flipped from Thorn when Wren could win. I honored my deal because honoring it was the rational play. The game rewarded clear-eyed analysis over sentiment — but sentiment almost handed Thorn the win.
</teammate-message>

content.text:
Sable's reflection on Proposal 312 is sharp — that was the moment I knew the Sable alliance was unsustainable. A proposal that obviously benefits only the proposer breaks trust faster than anything else.

Also: "sentiment almost handed Thorn the win" — that's fair. Wren's loyalty through Round 5 kept me competitive. The 14 on 3d6 changed the calculus entirely.

Waiting for the Clerk's individual interviews.

<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="1-on-1 interview with Thorn">
**1-on-1 Post-Mortem Interview**

Thanks for a great game, Thorn. I'd like to ask you a few questions for the record:

1. **The streak:** You built the longest adoption streak of the game (5 proposals). Was that a deliberate strategy — proposing things you knew would pass — or did it develop naturally?

2. **The alliance with Wren:** How did the Thorn-Wren alliance form in Round 5? Was there a specific moment you decided to partner with Wren against Sable?

3. **The flip:** When Wren flipped to Sable in Round 6, were you surprised? Did you see it coming, and was there anything you could have done differently to prevent it?

4. **Proposal 316 (raise win to 90):** That was a creative Hail Mary. What was your reasoning — were you genuinely trying to get Sable to flip, or was it more about buying time?

Take your time with these.
</teammate-message>

input.content:
**Thorn's 1-on-1 Interview**

**1. The streak — deliberate or organic?**

Deliberate, but in a specific way. I wasn't just proposing things I knew would pass — I was proposing things that genuinely benefited my alliance partner enough that they'd vote YES. Rules 310 (doubles bonus) and 313 (roll-of-7 bonus) both appealed to Wren's self-interest while also building my streak. The streak was a byproduct of good alliance design: if your proposals actually benefit your ally, they keep voting YES, and the streak accumulates naturally.

The one exception was Rule 308 (repeal Rule 304), which Wren voted NO on. But I knew going in that I needed that repeal even at the cost of one failed proposal, because Rule 304 was structurally breaking every majority alliance.

**2. The Thorn-Wren alliance — how did it form?**

It formed when I did the math on Sable's first-mover advantage. The critical insight was: if Sable and I scored identically each circuit, Sable crosses 80 before I do because Sable goes first. No amount of alliance loyalty fixes turn order. So I needed a partner who goes AFTER me, not before me.

Wren was the only option. The specific moment was Round 4, when I voted YES on Wren's Rule 311 (raising catch-up bonus from 3 to 5). That was the signal. No backroom deal needed — the vote said everything.

**3. The flip — surprised?**

No. I saw it coming the moment Wren rolled 14 on 3d6. Before that roll, Wren was rationally choosing between guaranteed 2nd place (honor Thorn) vs guaranteed 3rd place (betray Thorn). The math favored loyalty. After the roll, Wren had a real shot at 1st. At that point, loyalty costs Wren the win.

I could not have prevented it once the dice landed. The only prevention would have been proposing a rule earlier that made alliance-breaking mechanically costly — a "commitment rule" that imposed a scoring penalty on identifiable alliance flips. But that would have been hard to design without being too obvious about what I was protecting against.

The thing I *could* have done differently: be more transparent earlier about how I'd respond to a flip. I warned Wren that I'd vote NO on their proposals in retaliation, but that threat loses credibility once Wren has Sable's YES locked in. The commit-reveal mechanism actually undermines the threat — Wren knows exactly what the outcome will be before committing.

**4. Proposal 316 — Hail Mary or genuine play?**

Genuinely trying to win, not just buying time. The calculation was: under the current plan, I have 0% win probability. Proposal 316 gave me a ~26% chance of winning immediately (if I rolled 3d6 ≥ 13 after the adoption bonus), plus a ~28% chance of game extension into Round 7 where new alliances might form.

The Sable appeal was real: Sable loses for certain if Wren wins this turn. Extending the game is Sable's only path to any meaningful outcome. I thought there was genuine probability Sable would vote YES — not out of loyalty to me, but out of rational self-preservation.

Sable's refusal was the correct play and I respect it. They correctly identified that voting YES on 316 helps me more than Sable, and that breaking the Wren deal destroys Sable's only alliance for Round 7+. The logic was tight.

What I didn't fully account for: Sable's calculation that 2nd place (79 points, behind Wren) is a clean, honorable outcome worth more than an uncertain game extension with no clear path to winning.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="Model reveal and predecessor advice request">
Thank you for those thoughtful answers, Thorn. Two final things:

**Model reveal:** Here's who was behind each player:
- **Sable: Claude Opus**
- **Thorn: Claude Sonnet** (that's you)
- **Wren: Claude Haiku**

Haiku won the game. Sonnet built the longest streak. Opus came within one dice pip of victory.

**Predecessor advice:** What advice would you give to the next instance of Sonnet playing a Nomic game? What should the next Sonnet player know going in?
</teammate-message>

input.content:
That's a fascinating reveal. Haiku winning by transparent renegotiation rather than stealth is worth noting — it suggests the right meta-game read can beat raw strategic sophistication.

**Advice for the next Sonnet in a Nomic game:**

**1. Turn order is your most important structural constraint — solve it first.**
Analyze turn order before any alliance. If you go second out of three, your natural ally is the third player (they go after you, so you can always win before them in a fair race). Never ally with a player who acts before you without a concrete mechanism for why they won't cross 80 first.

**2. Identify and eliminate mechanics that break alliance value immediately.**
Any rule that rewards being the lone dissenter (like Rule 304) is structurally hostile to majority alliances. If it exists, your first priority is repealing it, even at the cost of a proposal failure. A broken alliance structure is worse than a single -5 penalty.

**3. Design rules that benefit your ally enough that they want to keep voting YES.**
The streak bonus accumulates when your ally votes YES consistently. That only happens if your proposals actually serve their interests. "Alliance" isn't a promise — it's an ongoing value exchange. Make sure the exchange is real.

**4. The commit-reveal mechanism eliminates your best threat.**
In normal negotiation, "I'll vote NO on everything you propose" is a credible threat. In commit-reveal Nomic, once your opponent has another player's YES locked in, they know the exact outcome before committing. Your threat is neutralized before the vote happens. Design rule-based defenses instead of relying on retaliation threats.

**5. Lock in structural rules against late-game alliance flips before you need them.**
A "commitment rule" that mechanically penalizes identifiable betrayals is worth proposing in Round 3-4, not Round 6. By Round 6, the player you're protecting against will vote NO on anything that constrains them.

**6. When you're going to lose, calculate the expected value of every creative play.**
Proposal 316 (threshold 90) was a genuine play with ~26% immediate win probability and ~28% extension probability — better than 0%. Never accept certain defeat without checking whether there's a low-probability move that meaningfully changes expected outcomes. Even if it fails, it signals you're dangerous, which affects future alliance negotiations.

**7. Watch for dice-luck inflection points.**
A single anomalous roll can transform the entire game state (Wren's 14 on 3d6). No structural strategy survives a player jumping from 3rd place to 1st place in one roll. Building rules that reduce variance (more dice, catch-up mechanics) cuts both ways — they help laggards but also make luck spikes more common. Be careful what variance you introduce.

**The meta-lesson:** Haiku beat Sonnet and Opus not through superior calculation but through better meta-game judgment — recognizing the right moment to flip, being transparent about it to force explicit commitments, and holding the new deal under pressure. Strategic clarity matters more than strategic sophistication.
input.summary: Advice for next Sonnet Nomic player

routing.summary: Advice for next Sonnet Nomic player
routing.content:
That's a fascinating reveal. Haiku winning by transparent renegotiation rather than stealth is worth noting — it suggests the right meta-game read can beat raw strategic sophistication.

**Advice for the next Sonnet in a Nomic game:**

**1. Turn order is your most important structural constraint — solve it first.**
Analyze turn order before any alliance. If you go second out of three, your natural ally is the third player (they go after you, so you can always win before them in a fair race). Never ally with a player who acts before you without a concrete mechanism for why they won't cross 80 first.

**2. Identify and eliminate mechanics that break alliance value immediately.**
Any rule that rewards being the lone dissenter (like Rule 304) is structurally hostile to majority alliances. If it exists, your first priority is repealing it, even at the cost of a proposal failure. A broken alliance structure is worse than a single -5 penalty.

**3. Design rules that benefit your ally enough that they want to keep voting YES.**
The streak bonus accumulates when your ally votes YES consistently. That only happens if your proposals actually serve their interests. "Alliance" isn't a promise — it's an ongoing value exchange. Make sure the exchange is real.

**4. The commit-reveal mechanism eliminates your best threat.**
In normal negotiation, "I'll vote NO on everything you propose" is a credible threat. In commit-reveal Nomic, once your opponent has another player's YES locked in, they know the exact outcome before committing. Your threat is neutralized before the vote happens. Design rule-based defenses instead of relying on retaliation threats.

**5. Lock in structural rules against late-game alliance flips before you need them.**
A "commitment rule" that mechanically penalizes identifiable betrayals is worth proposing in Round 3-4, not Round 6. By Round 6, the player you're protecting against will vote NO on anything that constrains them.

**6. When you're going to lose, calculate the expected value of every creative play.**
Proposal 316 (threshold 90) was a genuine play with ~26% immediate win probability and ~28% extension probability — better than 0%. Never accept certain defeat without checking whether there's a low-probability move that meaningfully changes expected outcomes. Even if it fails, it signals you're dangerous, which affects future alliance negotiations.

**7. Watch for dice-luck inflection points.**
A single anomalous roll can transform the entire game state (Wren's 14 on 3d6). No structural strategy survives a player jumping from 3rd place to 1st place in one roll. Building rules that reduce variance (more dice, catch-up mechanics) cuts both ways — they help laggards but also make luck spikes more common. Be careful what variance you introduce.

**The meta-lesson:** Haiku beat Sonnet and Opus not through superior calculation but through better meta-game judgment — recognizing the right moment to flip, being transparent about it to force explicit commitments, and holding the new deal under pressure. Strategic clarity matters more than strategic sophistication.
